data.title: Rigel Pharmaceuticals: 3-Drug Company Reaches Profitability
data.description:
When I last wrote about Rigel Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:RIGL) in late 2023, I rated it a strong buy due to a narrowing net loss, cheap valuation based on price/sales multiple and catalysts for its pipeline ahead. Since that time, RIGL has traded up roughly 60% with the company reporting a net income of $17.5M for 2024. An overhanging patent lawsuit with Annora Pharma concerning RIGL's biggest earner Tavalisse (fostamatinib), was a concern I noted at the time, although I expected that to take time to resolve. This article looks at recent developments for RIGL and the road ahead.

Tavalisse remains important

RIGL is a three-drug company with Tavalisse (fostamatinib), Rezlidhia (olutasidenib) and Gavreto (pralsetinib) as its marketed drugs. Tavalisse is still the big earner, however, accounting for two thirds ($31M) of RIGL's net product sales in Q4'24 ($46.5M).

The news on March 27, 2025, that RIGL had settled with Annora Pharma, in their patent litigation concerning Tavalisse, is important to digest. RIGL has agreed to let Annora sell a generic fostamatinib in the US in Q2'2032, or possibly earlier, which could mean RIGL might not face generic competition in the US for another seven years!

Under the terms of the settlement agreement, Annora will have a license to sell its generic product in Q2 2032 or earlier under certain circumstances. In accordance with the agreement, the parties terminated all ongoing litigation between Rigel and Annora regarding TAVALISSE patents pending in New Jersey.

RIGL press release March 27, 2025.

Language relating to "certain circumstances" allowing for an earlier entry to market is not uncommon when a company announces a settlement following a patent lawsuit. For example, such language was included in a January 2025, press release from Intra-Cellular Therapies (ITCI), when ITCI settled with Sandoz (OTCQX:SDZNY), allowing SDZNY to sell a generic Caplyta (lumateperone) in 2040.

The settlement agreement permits Sandoz to begin selling generic versions of CAPLYTA on July 1, 2040, or earlier under certain circumstances.

ITCI press release, January 10, 2025.

The possibility for earlier entry than the stated date in these agreements could refer to an "acceleration clause" or "most-favored entry clause." The exact terms of any such clause in the agreement between Annora and RIGL won't likely be known to us any time soon, but its mere presence shouldn't be of concern. Indeed, in January, the Federal Trade Commission issued a report, analyzing pharmaceutical patent settlement agreements from FY2018-FY2021, covered by the Medicare Prescription Drug, Improvement and Modernization Act of 2003 (MMA 2003). That report noted that in FY2021, the most recent year we have data from, of 199 settlements filed under the MMA 2003, 166 of them contained acceleration provisions.

Indeed, in the ITCI example above, the fact that the settlement with SDZNY covering Caplyta appeared to include some sort of acceleration provision didn't stop Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) from announcing it had agreed to acquire ITCI a few days later.

RIGL readouts ahead

RIGL is continuing on with development of R289 for lower-risk myelodysplastic syndromes (LR-MDS), but that work has been going on for years, such as initial trials of R835, of which R289 is a pro-drug, that began in 2018. Dose escalation of R289 has already seen some LR-MDS patients achieve transfusion independence, however, and so we're certainly at a point where RIGL might begin producing something more compelling to the market.

RIGL is currently dosing patients at 500 mg of R289, twice a day, and plans to start dose expansion work later in 2025. It looks like another readout from the phase 1b study can be expected in H2'25, so that represents a 2025 clinical catalyst for RIGL.

For our R289 program in lower-risk MDS, we expect to complete the dose escalation portion of the Phase Ib study. The new six dose level, 500 milligrams twice daily is actively enrolling. We then plan to initiate the dose expansion phase later this year. Also during the year, we plan to seek health authority input on the registrational path for R289 and we're anticipating presenting the dose escalation data from the Phase Ib study in the second half of the year.

Lisa Rojkjaer, Chief Medical Officer at RIGL, Q4'24 earnings call.

With olutasidenib, trials in various indications are now enrolling in concert with MD Anderson, but might take time to produce some data, RIGL hasn't provided much guidance on that front.

With the RIPK1 inhibitor program partnered with Eli Lilly and Co (LLY), LLY is running a trial of ocadusertib in an estimated 380 rheumatoid arthritis (RA) patients according to the clinicaltrials.gov listing. That study is randomized, double-blind and placebo-controlled, and has an efficacy endpoint at 12-weeks, so it should produce a fairly useful picture of how active ocadusertib is in RA patients. While the trial is still enrolling, it has an estimated primary completion date of February 2026, so it isn't years away from producing what could be a very big result for RIGL.

RIGL's Corporate presentation also mentions "in-licensing and product acquisition" as a potential strategy for development and expansion of its business (slide 3). With the company reporting a net income for 2024 and Q4'24 the possibility of in-licensing something to add to its three drugs is plausible, but whether or not that happens in 2025 remains to be seen. RIGL might prefer to see whether or not LLY chooses to develop ocadusertib further first, which could trigger milestone payments, making an in-licensing or acquisition even more feasible.

Financial Overview

RIGL reported total revenues of $57.6M in Q4'24 including $46.5M in net product sales and a further $11.1M in contract revenue from collaborations. Cost of product sales were $5.8M in Q4'24, R&D expenses were $5.6M for Q4'24 and SG&A expenses were $29.5M in the same quarter. RIGL reported a net income of $14.3M for the quarter or $0.81 per share.

As of December 31, 2024, RIGL had cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $77.3M. The company does have an outstanding credit facility with MidCap, with a principal balance of $60M, $7.5M of which is due in 2025.

Nonetheless, RIGL is guiding for 2025 total revenues of $200M-$210M, and net income for 2025. With that considered, RIGL's cash is not currently a concern, I don't see the company as likely to have to raise funds here.

There were 17,862,958 shares of RIGL's common stock outstanding as of February 25, 2025. RIGL has a market cap of $315.5M.

Conclusions, rating and risks

RIGL has reported a net income for the full year 2024, and for Q4'24. Even Tavalisse continues to grow, over six years from its launch. The recent settlement with Annora should help allay concerns over RIGL's dependency on Tavalisse currently. In any case, RIGL has the ability to continue to grow beyond Tavalisse with Gavreto off to a good start and Rezlidhia beginning key trials in new indications. There is also the RIPK1 inhibitor program in partnership with LLY that could greatly change RIGL's fortunes, given the size of the market for RA drugs. Considering RIGL's profitability, potential for further revenue growth, and the Annora settlement, I rate RIGL a buy. I stop short of rating the name a strong buy as RIGL could experience a dip in Q1 in net product sales, as it did in 2024, and Q1'25 earnings are a near-term event.

The risks of any long in RIGL are several fold, a few of which I'll discuss. Firstly, RIGL speaks of the possibility of in-licensing and product acquisition, and the company has done well before picking up Rezlidhia and Gavreto, but there are no guarantees that the market will be impressed by any subsequent in-licensing/acquisition.

Secondly, RIGL has reported sales growth of its marketed drugs over time, but the company still experiences seasonal variation in sales. A weak quarter could lead the market to question if RIGL can meet its 2025 revenue guidance.

Lastly, RIGL could report delays in starting or completing its clinical trials, as could its partners, such as LLY. Delays would move potential label expansion into the future and could also cause the stock to tumble.

data.title: Nasdaq Is In A Technical Bear Market: History Tells Us Where The Bottom Could Be
data.description:
Nobody could have expected what we've been experiencing in the last 48 hours: Liberation Day will go down in history for better or for worse. China responded to the U.S. tariff, and the result was once again devastating all over the world: The MSCI World Index is plummeting by over 4.20%.

Very few companies are holding up; technology companies have been the hardest hit.

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has plummeted 22% from its all-time high, and the S&P 500 (SP500) will probably follow suit. After all, the major U.S. tech companies represent 1/3 of the index and the well-known threshold of -20% from the high is quite close.

In addition to China's retaliatory tariffs, the market fears an even more aggressive response from Trump, who has already stated that “China played it wrong.” In all this, the European Union's reaction to the tariffs is still pending, and will probably come in the next few days. All in all, the trade war is heating up and heading towards an escalation.

Volatility is huge and investors are concerned about how quickly the situation is deteriorating. But how far can the U.S. stock market crash go? Nobody knows the answer, but we can get an idea based on what has happened in the past.

What history tells us and what I think

Firstly, a 20% drop does not necessarily translate into a recession, nor even two consecutive quarters of GDP contraction. The National Bureau of Economic Research decides when a recession starts and ends; there are many other economic aspects to consider, not just technical ones. It is important to specify this aspect because if the market collapses by 20%, it is not certain that there would be a recession that will lead to a collapse of a greater magnitude. We have a recent example of this, namely 2022.

The S&P 500 fell by 27%, but then began to rise again and there was no recession during that period. The index was simply too overvalued, and inflationary pressures combined with the Russo-Ukrainian war exacerbated the macroeconomic context. Everyone expected a recession, but it never happened, probably because the huge fiscal deficit supported economic growth.

However, let's assume that this time it's different, and that the United States is really heading towards a recession due to the trade war. How far could this collapse go? A study by Kathmere Capital Management helps us understand it.

Firstly, considering all the recessions since the 1950s, from its all-time high, the S&P 500 took an average of 13.60 months to reach its lowest point and fell by an average of 30.80%. Following these guidelines, the bottom should be reached on March 25, 2026, at 4,276 points, about 17% below current levels. A few weeks ago it seemed impossible, today a little less so.

However, considering all the recessions since the 1950s, the outliers of 1980 and 2020 could skew the final result. After all, Covid-19 was a flash crash and the performance of the S&P 500 in 2020 was very positive overall. I want to be as pessimistic as possible and consider only the most recent and severe recessions, i.e., 2001 and 2007. In this case, the bottom will be reached on February 10, 2027, at 2,887 points. This means that the S&P 500 must lose a further 43%, so we're really only just getting started. Obviously, this is an almost impossible scenario, it would require a black swan event.

However, there is some positive news in all this: two years after the recession began, you have probably already recovered a significant part of your losses.

On average, two years after the start of a recession, the S&P 500 is up 32%. There may be very negative periods, such as in 2007 and 2001, but if you're patient enough and average the price down during the crash, you'll reach break-even faster. In other words, by investing during the downturn and keeping your nerve, it's just a matter of several months/quarters. It will certainly be a stressful period, but it will end: bear markets tend to last much less than bull markets.

Finally, let's take a look at the Shiller PE Ratio, a famous indicator of market valuation. I will consider the last 20 years since I don't think it makes much sense to compare the current stock market with that of the last century.

As you can see, we are still at a rather high level, but today's sharp drop has not been counted. Even including it, we are above average and still far from the levels reached in 2022. Frankly, I don't think we'll ever reach 15x like during the GFC, reaching 25x would already be a decent level to believe that the bottom has been reached.

I don't have a crystal ball and I can't know where the bottom will be, but I have marked four potential levels based on different scenarios. They are just personal considerations, nothing more.

  • The first scenario is that the bottom is at the current level, and the market will start to recover in the coming weeks. Trump will back down on the tariffs, as will China, and the European Union will try to negotiate without adding fuel to the fire.
  • The second scenario, which I consider more likely, is that Trump will continue along these lines for a while longer, and the European Union could come into play with a plan of retaliatory tariffs. At that point, a new collapse will be triggered, causing the S&P 500 to lose 21%.

    After a few weeks of negotiations everything will be resolved as far as tariffs are concerned.

  • The third scenario, less likely than the previous one but perhaps more likely than the first, is that the S&P 500 will collapse by 30% to 4,300 points. In this scenario, the trade war must escalate, and the European Union must enact a particularly disadvantageous tariff plan against the United States. At that point, there must be an even more aggressive reaction from Trump, both against the European Union and against China. The whole thing will last several months.
  • The fourth scenario, the least likely, is the same as the third, except that it will last longer. The tariffs will be in place for several quarters without any agreement being reached between the United States and the targeted countries, and this will significantly weaken the U.S. economy. In this scenario, the risk of stagflation is high, as the tariffs create inflationary pressure. The Fed won't even be able to reduce rates and the S&P 500 will lose 40%.

I don't think a scenario worse than the fourth is feasible, but anything can happen. The only certainty is that, even if this were to be a bear market, sooner or later, there will be a recovery. Investing gradually during a bear market guarantees a faster recovery, and before the S&P 500 reaches new highs, you'll already be in profit. Usually, two years of patience is more than enough to not even realize that there has been a recession along the way.

data.title: Nike: Reciprocal Tariffs More Likely To Change 'Win Now' Strategy To 'Win Never'
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about NIKE, Inc. (NYSE:NKE), back in July 2024, I analysed the company’s Q4 earnings report and argued how the company’s innovation strategy had nothing to show for, and why the company was risking falling further behind the competition. I had a HOLD rating on the stock.

Since the article was published, the stock has tumbled nearly 24%, significantly underperforming the S&P 500, which has declined 7% during the same period.

In this article, I analyse the extent of the impact that the recent announcement of the reciprocal tariffs would have on the company’s long-term growth, and whether its turnaround strategy can shield it from some tariff-related headwinds.

Nike’s Turnaround Attempt Just Got A Whole Lot Harder

If one carefully listened to NKE’s Q3 earnings call, then it becomes very clear that the management really tried their best to demonstrate a lot of optimism about the company’s future. New CEO, Elliott Hill, about 6 months into the job, talked about how, after studying the current state of the company, he and his team have outlined a new turnaround strategy dubbed “Win Now.” The strategy would prioritize five core areas (running, basketball, football, training, and sportswear), and across three countries (U.S., China, and the United Kingdom) in general, and five cities (New York, Los Angeles, London, Beijing, and Shanghai) in particular. The ‘Win Now’ strategy will also focus on a complete product refresh by focusing on new product launches, in order to offset the continuous declines seen in the company’s flagship products such as Air Force 1 and Jordan 1. Finally, the strategy also focuses on a clear goal of rebuilding relationships with the company’s wholesale partners, and refining its marketplace presence, especially in China, in order to improve customer engagement.

The most recent quarter of NKE came in better-than-expected, with a beat on both the top and bottom lines, but all key metrics showed significant declines. I am particularly concerned about gross margins, which were down 330 bps (3.3%) and diluted EPS, which were down by a whopping 30% y/y. Moreover, while inventories were down 2% y/y, they still came in at $7.5 billion, which suggests that NKE has a lot of work ahead to clear out their old merchandise before they can even consider scaling up their new products. There was some indication that this might be the case during the Q3 earnings call, when management announced that the production of Pegasus Premium is only expected to scale up by the fall of 2025, and new additions to the Vomero franchise are only expected to arrive at some time during next fiscal year. Finally, the pain from China continued, as revenues declined 15% y/y on a constant currency basis, and EBIT in the region declined by 42% y/y.

Despite these dismal figures, and despite issuing softer-than-expected guidance, the management, during the earnings call, was buzzing with optimism and reiterated their confidence in the success of the Win Now strategy. This is not the first time that NKE management has demonstrated irrational optimism, and it is unfortunate that despite being in a harsher macro environment today, the optimism doesn’t seem to subside.

Investors, in my opinion, shouldn’t fall for this optimism, especially now that the reciprocal tariffs have been announced by the Trump administration. These tariffs have, according to me, made NKE’s turnaround strategy likely to be dead on arrival. There are multiple reasons behind my thesis. Firstly, the company’s guidance for Q4 did not take into account the reciprocal tariffs, which came in much harsher than expected. Q4 gross margins, for instance, are expected to be down 400 to 500 bps (4% to 5%) y/y, as a result of the transition to the Win Now strategy. The gross margins guidance only factored in the initial tariffs that were imposed on China and Mexico. Since then, the tariffs on China have been bumped up from 20% to 54%. To make matters worse, approximately 80% of Nike’s imports to the United States come from Vietnam, which just got slapped with a 46% tariff. Another 6% of Nike’s imports to the United States come from Indonesia, which got slapped with 32% reciprocal tariffs. While only about 7% of Nike's imports to the U.S. come from China, the new 54% tariffs make it a costly affair. It is, therefore, not surprising to me that the company tanked 14.5% post the reciprocal tariffs announcement.

I am not saying that NKE's management should have accurately predicted the extent of the Trump Administration’s reciprocal tariffs. Nobody anticipated that they would come in harsher-than-expected, especially based on the signals that White House officials were sending in the days prior to the announcement. But to proclaim that the 'Win Now' strategy’s success is a foregone conclusion when there was also the possibility that the macroenvironment would be getting worse, should not offer any reassurance to investors. Moreover, the demand creation expense for the latest quarter was up 8% y/y, which makes the level of confidence that the management has placed on this strategy, even more shocking. NKE wants to win now. However, the extent of reciprocal tariffs on the company’s major import locations should make investors wonder investors whether NKE would ever win.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Target Price

$43

Forward P/E

17.2x

Projected FY25 Diluted EPS

$2.34

Forward PEG Ratio

3.36

Projected FY26 EPS Growth

5.12%

Projected FY26 EPS

$2.46

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), Seeking Alpha, Author’s Calculations & NKE Q3FY25 Earnings Call.

The company now expects Q4 revenues to be down mid-teens range, “albeit at the low-end.” With the macroeconomic environment at its worst in a while for consumer discretionary names, I have assumed Q4 revenues to decline 16% y/y, higher than management’s own guidance. This would result in projected Q4 revenues to be $10.59 billion.

The company now expects gross margins in Q4 to decline to 400 to 500 bps. The guidance doesn’t take into account the impact of reciprocal tariffs, and as such, I have assumed FY25 gross margins to decline by 500 bps y/y, which would result in projected gross margins of 39.7% for Q4, subsequently resulting in Q4 gross profits of $4.2 billion.

The company expects Q4 SG&A expenses to be up low to mid-single digits. I have assumed a 2% increase, resulting in Q4 SG&A expenses of $3.78 billion. Other income and expenses are expected to be $45 million to $55 million. I have assumed Q4's other income and expenses, including net interest income to be $55 million. I have assumed the effective tax rate to be 15.7% for Q4. Combining all these figures would result in Q4 of net income after taxes of $0.475 billion. At 1.481 billion diluted shares outstanding, Q4 diluted EPS is projected to come in at $0.32. This results in a total projected FY25 Diluted EPS of $2.34.

According to LSEG Data, the company currently trades at a forward P/E of 26.9x, making it the most expensive among its peers. NKE is a market leader but faces significantly more challenges in the current climate compared to some of its other peers such as LULU. For instance, LULU’s China business is doing remarkably well compared to NKE. And while both companies have exposure to countries like Vietnam, the fact that LULU’s turnaround story is at a more advanced level compared to NKE, which has barely got started suggests that it doesn’t deserve to trade at current multiples. In my most recent article on LULU, I put a forward P/E of 22.8x for the company. NKE’s trailing P/E stands at 17.2x, according to LSEG Data. I find this to be a more reasonable assumption under the current climate. Therefore, I have assumed this multiple for my calculations.

NKE currently trades at a forward PEG ratio of 11.23, according to Seeking Alpha, which makes it significantly overvalued relative to the industry median (1.26) and relative to its own 5-year historical average (3.36). At its historical 5-year forward PEG ratio of 3.36, EPS growth would come in at 5.12%, and FY26 EPS is subsequently projected to come in at $2.46.

A forward P/E of 17.2x and a projected EPS of $2.46 would result in a price target of $43, which suggests that the stock is currently overvalued by nearly 23% at current levels. To make matters worse, the company’s momentum is significantly broken, as seen by the D+ grade on Seeking Alpha's Momentum scale. Combining all of this, I am downgrading the stock from a HOLD to a SELL.

Risk Factors

The main risk to my bear case is that the impact of the tariffs would turn out to be relatively lower compared to what is currently being expected. At the time of writing, news has been trickling in, which mentions that President Trump has had positive talks with Vietnam, and this could see tariffs being lowered on the country. This is a positive, and if implemented, could see the company getting a significant boost to its margins.

The other risk factor is that the company can clear its existing inventory at lower markdowns than current expectations. Once again this would boost margins and subsequently the company’s bottom line.

Concluding Thoughts

NKE has undergone quite a transformation since I last wrote about the company. CEO John Donahoe is no longer at the helm, and there is a real sense of emergency from the current management about undertaking a long-overdue product refresh. The Q3 earnings call was one where the new CEO Elliott Hill was expressing a lot of confidence in the company’s latest turnaround strategy, dubbed ‘Win Now.’ While the strategy sounds promising, there is a long way to go before the company can get back on the growth track in my opinion. The existing products continued to be sold at a discount, thereby affecting the company’s margins, and the $7.5 billion in inventory suggests that there is a long way to go before they can be cleared. While the new products have been well-received, they are not likely to change the company’s trajectory in the near term.

And then there’s the reciprocal tariffs, the impact of which, the management simply hasn’t considered in the guidance. These tariffs have come in far worse than expected, as a result of which, the company’s future growth is likely to be adversely affected. From a valuation standpoint, the stock continues to be overvalued even at current levels. What is so surprising to see is the hyper-optimism that the management continues to exude, despite all these headwinds. The last time such levels of misplaced optimism were projected, the CEO ended up losing his job. Existing investors must have a sense of déjà vu, especially after the Q3 earnings call.

data.title: Sangamo Therapeutics: Scope Goes Beyond That Of Recent Eli Lilly Licensing Deal
data.description:
Sangamo Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SGMO) just the other day announced that it had been able to establish a deal with Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) in order to advance up to 5 neurological target indications. This big pharma specifically wants to develop genomic medicines to be delivered to the central nervous system [CNS] efficiently using Sangamo's proprietary STAC-BBB intravenously delivered capsid. This deal is another good one for this small-cap biotech because it brings about an upfront payment of about $18 million, and it is then eligible to earn up to $1.4 billion in licensed target fees and milestone payments across 5 target indications. The stock gapped higher the other day in after-hours trading on the back of the release of such news, but today it is trading flat with the broader market.

However, I believe that there is another even more important reason to go over this company now. Not just because it established a third deal with a big pharmaceutical company to develop the STAC-BBB AAV capsid to treat a wide variety of disorders. I believe that while this capsid is primed to deliver strong growth in the future if it can be delivered into the CNS of humans effectively, there is a better near-term opportunity that investors should keep an eye on. This would be in terms of the development of its Fabry Disease program, which uses gene therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec to treat these patients. The reason why investors should care about this program now is because there is a major catalyst event on the horizon which is going to have a huge impact on changing the scope of the company's value.

This would be in terms of the fact that it expects to release data from the phase 1/2 STAAR study, using isaralgagene civaparvovec to treat these Fabry Disease patients, in the 1st half of 2025. The significance of this is that the FDA already agreed that 52-week estimated glomerular filtration rate [eGFR] data of this caliber could be used as an interim endpoint so that it could file for Accelerated Approval of this gene therapy. If the data pans out, then the company notes that it will be able to file a Biologics License Application [BLA] to the FDA in the 2nd half of 2025. Another catalyst is that the company continues to engage in business development discussions so that it could possibly establish a partnership to help commercialize it just in case it is ultimately approved for U.S. marketing.

Fabry Disease Program Remains On Track With 1st Half 2025 Data Expected

As I stated above, while Sangamo likely has a good pathway forward with several of the big pharma collaboration agreements it had established for the advancement of its proprietary STAC-BBB AAV Capsid, it has a near-term catalyst that is likely to drive shareholder value. Of course, that's if the program ends up with a positive clinical outcome. This would be in terms of the development of gene therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease, which is being explored in the ongoing phase 1/2 STAAR study. Before going over recent data from this mid-stage trial, plus any catalysts to be released for this program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Fabry Disease [FD] is characterized as a rare genetic disorder by which these patients are unable to produce a specific enzyme that is important for breaking down a fat-like substance in the patient's body known as sphingolipids. This is classified as a lysosomal storage disorder in which patients lack the important enzyme of alpha-galactosidase A [alpha-GAL]. Why is the lack of this enzyme a very bad thing? It is because without this enzyme in place to break down such fats, then they are deposited on the tissues and blood vessels in many parts of the body. Such parts of the body that could be affected by Fabry Disease are as follows:

  • Kidneys.
  • Brain.
  • Central Nervous System.
  • Skin.
  • Heart.

The global Fabry Disease market is expected to grow to $5.17 billion by 2034. This is indeed a very large market opportunity and as I will explain below, the company might actually have an Accelerated Approval pathway, depending upon what happens next with the ongoing trial.

In order to see whether or not Sangamo Therapeutics will be able to effectively use isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of these patients with Fabry Disease, it is in the process of running the ongoing phase 1/2 STAAR study. This specific trial is set to recruit up to a total of 34 patients who are to receive a single dose of this gene therapy to treat their disease. The way this study was set up was to look at safety primarily at first, over a 12-month period. However, there is an efficacy endpoint that is being evaluated for this particular trial, which is the eGFR slope at 52 weeks. Before going over initial data from this study, I believe it is important to note two things here, which are why this endpoint is being done and the reason why it could be used to move isaralgagene civaparvovec towards the possibility of filing a BLA of it in the 2nd half of 2025.

A problem with Fabry Disease is that the fatty deposits on tissues and blood vessels of organs and other parts of the body cause damage. One hallmark problem that occurs in these patients is that without proper treatment, these patients can have end-stage renal disease [ESRD]. In order to measure the stabilization of kidney disease for these patients, the eGFR slope is utilized. A therapy is considered effective when it can slow the decline to a certain point and then ineffective when the slope dramatically falls off. That is, there is the occurrence of eGFR loss that remains ≥3 ml/min/1.73 m2/year (≥2.5 ml/min/1.73 m2/year in females).

It was revealed in a follow-up of 23 patients who completed 12 months of treatment with isaralgagene civaparvovec that they had seen a positive mean estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) slope of 3.061 mL/min/1.73m2/year. In essence, it indicated that these patients experienced notable improvements in renal function. What remains to be seen now is that longer follow-up, plus the addition of other patients, establishes a stabilization of less of a decline in eGFR. Only if this company gets positive data can it hope to be able to file a BLA of this gene therapy for the treatment of these Fabry Disease patients.

The thing is that the endpoint to be looked at under the Accelerated Approval pathway will be the eGFR slope at 52 weeks. The company had to work with the FDA to get to this point, of allowing this to be an intermediate clinical endpoint that would allow for it to file for Accelerated Approval of this therapy for the treatment of these patients. That is, it met with the FDA in a Type B interaction, whereby the agency stated that such 52-week data from this phase 1/2 STAAR study would be enough to serve as the ability for it to file its BLA. Other positives established from this particular study is that 18 out of 18 patients remain off enzyme replacement therapy [ERT]. Plus, sustained increase of alpha-GAL for up to 47 months in the longest patient treated.

This is where the major catalyst comes into play, which, I believe, will be a huge inflection point for this company. That is, it is expected to release eGFR slope 52-week data from the phase 1/2 STAAR study in these Fabry Disease patients, in the 1st half of 2025. The hope is that isaralgagene civaparvovec will be able to stabilize or improve patients in terms of this eGFR slope efficacy measure. If the slope has a massive decline and such patients aren't stabilized, then that could spell an end to this clinical program.

Should the data turn out to be positive, then the company expects to file its BLA for this gene therapy in the 2nd half of 2025. Another potential catalyst, which may or may not happen, would be that it is looking to establish a collaboration agreement with a big pharmaceutical partner to help commercialize this drug. If this happens, then this is another key event that might cause the stock price to trade higher. Plus, it would take care of Sangamo's cash concerns as well.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Sangamo Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents of $41.9 million in cash of $41.9 million as of December 31st, 2024. The thing is that despite this cash on hand noted, plus additional cash of $10.1 million from an ATM in 2025 and $5 million end of March from Pfizer Inc. (PFE) for $5 million, it doesn't have an extensive cash runway. With this, it guided that it would only have enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least until the middle of Q2 of 2025.

Truth be told, the deal the other day with Eli Lilly only brought in an upfront payment of $18 million. Barring another unforeseen deal regarding the Fabry Disease program or other program in its pipeline, it may need to raise cash. It could probably even do so after the release of data from the phase 1/2 STAAR study targeting patients with Fabry Disease.

The company burns roughly $33.5 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $23.6 million in R&D expenses and then $9.9 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Sangamo Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the collaboration agreement it had made with several big pharma companies to develop its proprietary STAC-BBB AAV capsid. The belief is that this intravenously delivered capsid can cross the blood-brain barrier and achieve transduction in the brain in a more efficient manner in the CNS. The risk here is that there is no assurance that these findings will be replicated in human clinical trials, nor such capsids will be able to achieve success in such ongoing studies. With this capsid failing to achieve proof-of-concept, this means that Sangamo would lose out on being able to obtain milestone payments and royalties on future net sales of products marketed. Not only that, but such trial failures would cause the stock price to trade lower.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the Accelerated Approval pathway of the Fabry Disease program, which I went over above. The hope is that isaralgagene civaparvovec as gene therapy will be effective in being able to help these patients in the ongoing phase 1/2 STAAR study. In particular, the goal is to achieve the intermediate endpoint of the eGFR slope at 52 weeks. The risk here is that upon the release of data, there is no guarantee that it will end up being positive. Even if a BLA filing does happen for isaralgagene civaparvovec for these patients, there is no assurance that the FDA will grant marketing approval for it upon review.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the advancement of giroctocogene fitelparvovec for the treatment of patients with moderate-to-severe Hemophilia A. Pfizer chose to terminate its collaboration agreement with Sangamo for this program. The hope is that this small-cap biotech can find another partner that is willing to fund it going forward. The risk here is that there is no assurance that it will end up finding a partner who is willing to continue to advance it. Another risk to consider is that upon no suitors being found to fund it, it could just choose to end the program entirely.

Conclusion

Sangamo Therapeutics has done well to develop the use of its gene therapy isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease. It is nearing the finish line for this program and the upcoming eGFR slope 52-week data in the 1st half of 2025 will be a huge inflection point. In order for the other two catalysts to happen, they have to be positive. With positive data, that will first lead to the ability to file a BLA of this gene therapy to treat these patients. Secondly, the company would then still be inclined to establish a partnership to help commercialize this drug. Beyond the scope of the Fabry Disease, Hemophilia A treatment programs, and the collaborations with big pharma for the STAC-BBB capsid, it has an extensive pipeline with another candidate.

One such other candidate would be ST-503 which is being developed to treat pain due to idiopathic small fiber neuropathy [iSFN]. This gene therapy specifically targets the SCN9A gene and the purpose of this is to reduce the expression of NAV1.7 sodium channels in sensory neurons. In essence, with this being fully established, it is supposed to have a huge impact on reducing pain hypersensitivity after patients only receive one intrathecal administration of ST-503. With the potential for Accelerated Approval of isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease based on the intermediate clinical endpoint, plus several big pharma deals signed to develop its proprietary STAC-BBB AAV capsid, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cincinnati Financial: Being A Dividend King Not Enough To Offset Cyclical Headwinds
data.description:
Cincinnati Financial Corporation (NASDAQ:CINF) is an insurance company with a great dividend history, but this doesn't seem to be a big support for its share price ahead. As this is my first coverage on the company, in this article I review its business profile and most recent earnings, to see if CINF is an attractive play for long-term investors or not in the insurance sector.

Business Overview

CINF operates as a property and casualty (P&C) insurance company, being among the 25 largest P&C carriers in the country based on annual net written premiums. The company is based in Ohio and was founded in 1950, having therefore a long history in the insurance industry. Its current market value is about $22 billion, being a mid-sized insurance company by this measure.

While its core business is providing P&C insurance, it also offers life insurance, leasing and financing services to its customers. As shown in the next graph, the company's revenues are well diversified by insurance lines, with commercial lines being the largest business segment.

Regarding its business model, CINF operates mainly through independent insurance agents, which distribute its products and services throughout 46 states. In the life insurance segment, it also offers leasing and financing services, which is not usual among P&C companies. The company believes that by operating in this way, it can be more profitable and grow sustainably over the long term, leaving significant decision-making to local subsidiaries that can better adapt product offering and underwriting to specific market needs.

Regarding its growth strategy, CINF aims to offer a good service to agents, expecting to build long-term relationships and gain more market share within its selected agents. On top of business growth, the company also seeks to operate profitably in the insurance segment, by having strong underwriting expertise and knowledge which should lead to adequate pricing throughout market cycles.

Historically, this strategy has been sound given that CINF's growth history is positive, reporting a compounded annual growth rate (CGAR) of 8% on P&C premiums over the past 15 years, beating the industry's growth during this period. On the other hand, its combined ratio was 96% on average over the past 15 years, which is not an impressive outcome compared to some of its peers.

Indeed, as I've covered in previous articles, there are P&C companies that consistently report combined ratios near or below 90% over the long term, such as Chubb (CB) or Progressive (PGR), showing that CINF does not stand out in the P&C industry by its underwriting capabilities. Nevertheless, CINF has been able to report underwriting profits over the past few years that were better than the industry average, but due to a relatively low underwriting profitability, most of its value creation in the past has been generated by financial investments rather than its core insurance business.

As shown in the previous graph, CINF's historical value creation has been driven mainly by equity portfolio gains and investment income, while P&C underwriting has relatively a small contribution to value creation. This happens because CINF has a different asset allocation than most insurance companies, investing a large part of its investment portfolio in equities. Indeed, about 40% of its allocation is to equities, while usually insurance companies are much more conservative in their investment allocation, preferring to invest most of their investments in low-risk assets classes, such as sovereign, municipal, or credit bonds. Usually, fixed-income securities represent between 80-90% of insurance companies' investment allocation, while riskier asset classes have much lower weights.

This is clearly a distinctive factor for CINF compared to other insurance companies, making it to some extent comparable to Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), that also invests considerable own funds in equities. Moreover, CINF's investment style also seems to be biased to value investing, even though it also has sizable holdings in companies like Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) or Broadcom (AVGO) in the technology sector. Nevertheless, its large exposure to equities makes its investment case more geared to the capital markets than most of its peers, which can be a double-edge sword, depending on the stock market being on a bull or bear market trend.

In recent years, this profile has not been particularly positive, as CINF's share price returns have been lower than compared to the P&C industry in the U.S. and the overall stock market, as shown in the next graph. This should raise some questions about its business model and investment allocation, as the company has been a laggard compared to peers.

However, the company is not expected to change much its business strategy going forward given that its main targets are to invest in its insurance business, invest more in equity securities, deliver a growing dividend, repurchase shares if capitalization remains sound, and eventually pursue M&A transactions if attractive opportunities arise.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, CINF has reported a positive operating momentum in the recent past as the P&C market has been on a "hard" cycle, which means pricing has improved and claims have remained relatively low, leading to improved profitability for most players in the industry.

CINF was no exception and reported higher premiums, investment income, and earnings last year, while its book value reached a new record high. This positive performance was supported both by its insurance business and positive investment returns, showing that is operating backdrop was quite positive during 2024.

Indeed, its total revenues amounted to nearly $11.4 billion last year, up by 13% YoY, driven both by higher net written premiums (+12% YoY to $8.9 billion) and investment income (+15% YoY to more than $1 billion). Regarding investment income, the company reached a significant milestone given that it surpassed more than $1 billion for the first time, as CINF investment portfolio has increased both due to higher earnings and positive returns on the capital markets over the past couple of years, to more than $28 billion at the end of 2024.

Regarding its insurance operations, profitability improved due to higher premiums growth than loss and underwriting expenses growth during the past year, resulting in an underwriting profit of $580 million in 2024 (+45% YoY). Its combined ratio was 93.4% last year (vs. 94.9% in 2023), a positive trend that accelerated in the last two quarters of the year as premium growth was clearly higher than loss expenses. In Q4 2024, its combined ratio improved to 84.7%, showing that its underwriting profitability improved markedly toward the year-end. Its net income increased by 24% YoY to nearly $2.3 billion last year, of which close to $1.1 billion were capital gains related to its investment portfolio.

While this was a positive performance and the operating landscape remains favorable for insurance companies, the company was exposed to the LA wildfires back in January, which will impact negatively its underwriting result in Q1 2025. The company's guidance is to report a pre-tax catastrophe loss between $450-525 million net of recoveries, being a significant hit for the company's loss ratio in 2025. Indeed, if this event happened last year, the company would still make an underwriting profit, but it would be quite low. This shows that LA wildfires were a significant event and the company's underwriting profitability will be much lower in 2025 compared to its recent history.

Indeed, according to analysts' estimates, CINF's combined ratio is expected to be around 99.2% in 2025. Thus, the company is expected to report only a small underwriting profit this year, being a significant headwind for earnings growth in the short term. Moreover, the company's relatively high exposure to equities is also concerning given recent market volatility and declining prices in recent weeks, boding badly for investment gains in the first half of 2025. While current consensus seems to reflect higher catastrophe losses, considering that CINF is expected to report a net loss of $40 million in Q1 2025, lower equity prices doesn't seem to be reflected year as book value is expected to grow in Q2, something that may be difficult to achieve due to recent weakness in the stock market that will impact the company's comprehensive income.

Despite these cyclical headwinds and prospects of lower earnings in 2025, the company has recently raised its dividend. Investors can see this as a sign that management is confident about the business outlook, but this seems to be more a reflection of its past performance rather than being particularly bullish about its financial figures ahead. Moreover, investors should consider that CINF is a dividend king, which means it has raised its dividend for more than 50 years consecutively, a trend that its management is clearly committed to maintain regardless of its earnings path.

As shown in the next graph, its dividend history seems to be more a "political" commitment rather than a reflection of its earnings, as the company raised its dividend even during years when it reported negative earnings, showing that the relationship between earnings and dividends can be questionable in the short term.

Having said that, its dividend has been covered by earnings over the long term, given that its dividend payout ratio was only 29% on average over the last decade. Thus, its dividend seems to be sustainable, and I'm not expecting the company to give up on its dividend growth policy ahead, but investors should be aware that its fantastic dividend history is not much likely to support its share price in the short term. Furthermore, its dividend yield is only about 2.5%, which is not particularly impressive, and therefore I don't expect its terrific dividend history to be enough to offset cyclical headwinds in the coming quarters.

Regarding its valuation, CINF is currently trading at around 1.5x book value, which is in-line with its historical valuation over the past five years. This means its shares appear to be fairly valued right now, following a 10% correction over the past few days due to the trade tariffs drama that is clearly not specific to CINF.

Conclusion

Cincinnati Financial Corporation has reported a positive operating performance over the past couple of years, but this trend is expected to change in 2025 due to higher catastrophe losses and recent market turmoil is also negative for its investment portfolio. While some investors may see recent market weakness as a buying opportunity, I don't see CINF as being undervalued and don't think it offers much value for long-term investors right now.

data.title: Maravai LifeSciences: Upside Not Likely In 2025, But Company Can Survive
data.description:
Investment Overview

In my last note on Maravai LifeSciences Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVI) back in October 2023, I discussed the company's $1.62bn initial public offering in November 2020, its "Clean Cap" analog technology, which helps make messenger-RNA more stable, and its incorporation into Pfizer Inc. (PFE) and BioNTech SE's (BNTX) "Comirnaty" Covid vaccine, which helped it report revenues of $799m in 2021, and $883m in 2022.

This success helped Maravai grow its share price from its IPO value of $27 per share, to a high of >$60 per share in August 2021, valuing the business at $6.5bn. Naturally however, the end of the pandemic led to a drastic reduction in sales of Comirnaty, and Maravai's revenues in 2023, and, noting that "CleanCap COVID vaccine revenues were responsible for ~68% of Maravai's total revenues in 2022, and ~70% in 2021," and despite the fact Maravai stock had slipped from $60 per share, to $6 per share, I opted for a "Sell" rating.

This has proven to be the right call, as Maravai stock now trades at $2.1 per share, down >65% since my note. Despite this, I am curious to see if Maravai may be able to find a way to strategize itself out of its current slump, and find new markets and uses for its pioneering technology. Hence, I am revisiting the company and providing some updated thoughts.

Maravai In 2025

As we can see below (slide from Maravai 2024 annual report/10-K submission), Maravai operates two core businesses, in Nucleic Acid Production, and Biologics Safety Testing:

Nucleic acid production accounted for 76% of the $259m of revenues Maravai reported in 2024. On its Q4 2024 earnings call with analysts, the company's Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Kevin Herde told analysts:

Breaking down our full-year revenues by customer type, we estimate that 48% of our 2024 revenue was from biopharma, 25% from life science and diagnostics, 5% from CROs, CMOs, and CDMOs, 4% from academia, and roughly 18% of our revenue was shipped through distributors, including to the customer categories I just mentioned.

The San Diego, California-based company reported its Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings, and filed its 10-K on March 18th after a delay caused by accounting issues. In Q4, Nucleic Acid production revenues fell by 29% year-on-year, to $42m, and Biologics Safety Testing revenues also fell, by 4%, to $14.7m.

Net loss for the year was $(145m) on a GAAP basis, or $(1.05) per share, while adjusted net loss came to $(25m), or $(0.1) per share, which excludes a goodwill impairment charge of $166.2m, among other items - Maravai's accounting looks undeniably ugly at the present time. Cash position was reported as $322m, and long-term gross debt position as $300m

Regarding 2025 guidance, management commented as follows on the Q4 earnings call:

We are focused on returning our base business to growth. We anticipate our base business, which excludes high volume CleanCap to be about $185 million to $205 million or to grow in the low-single digits at the midpoint.

We currently do not have any binding commitments from our top customers for high volume CleanCap demand for 2025. Thus, we believe it to be prudent to guide only to our base business, as discussed, without incorporating any high volume CleanCap into our initial 2025 revenue guidance. To the extent commitments are received for high volume CleanCap throughout the year, we will incorporate those into any guidance updates as we progress through 2025.

As a result of the overall revenue guidance and expectations here of $185 million to $205 million, we do not anticipate being in a positive adjusted EBITDA position at these levels, and thus we are not providing guidance for that profitability metric in 2025 at this stage.

High volume Clean Cap accounted for ~$66m of revenues in 2024, and is primarily related to supplying products for COVID vaccines - on the earnings call, when asked if "COVID vaccine contributions have been scrubbed from the forecast entirely," management replied:

Yeah, I mean, we think it's basically zeroed down. I mean, we're taking out everyone that has a commercialized vaccine in their demand. They're not giving us any forecast, so we're by default taking it out. As you know, we've never had a year where that's roughly been less than $60 million going back to 2020, so it's certainly unique to have zero. We don't feel there's anything else out there.

This essentially confirms that the element of revenues that excited the market, and allowed Maravai stock to surge during the pandemic years, will not return, and that the business must focus its energies elsewhere. In that sense, a current market cap valuation of $503m (at the time of writing) for a ~$200m revenue company that expects to be loss-making in 2025 is fair-to-high, I'd speculate. Is there anything management can do to change the narrative going forward?

Looking Ahead - Plenty Of Capacity, Fewer Customers

In essence, Maravai can only be successful if its clients have a high demand for the products it can produce - Maravai says in its 10K:

mRNA lies at the core of our capabilities and expertise. We have developed significant proficiency in mRNA technology, driven by our belief in its transformative potential as a therapeutic modality. The first clinical trial involving an mRNA therapeutic agent took place in 2016. Since then, over 1,500 clinical trials are now in the pipeline, encompassing a wide range of medical applications.

Unfortunately, although the number of ongoing studies figure seems large, mRNA medicines have not progressed as had been hoped during the pandemic, when it was generally believed that post-COVID, this approach could be applied successfully to a host of new indications.

If we think about mRNA specialists, Moderna, Inc. (MRNA) has successfully commercialized a single product since the pandemic, an RSV vaccine whose future earning potential appears to be restricted by competition - Pfizer Inc. (PFE) and AstraZeneca also has RXV vaccines on the market - and a vaccine sceptic new healthcare administration in the U.S.

BioNTech has released no new mRNA products, and neither has Pfizer. Although Maravai says that "mRNA-based programs are addressing various medical conditions," including in oncology, liver disease, cardiovascular disease, and autoimmune conditions, other vaccines, and even cell and gene therapy programs, the lack of progress to date, I would argue, is a long-term concern for Maravai.

Not only that, but Maravai's operates within a competitive marketplace. The company name checks the likes of Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO), Aldeveron, New England Biolabs, Lonza, Catalent - now part of Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), Qiagen N.V. (QGEN), Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY), and Samsung Biologics as competitors. Most if not all of these companies are better resourced than Maravai, and if one product falls out of use, are in a better position to develop new products or pivot towards other products in their portfolio.

On the plus side, Maravai says, "We occupy facilities in San Diego, California, Leland, North Carolina, Sterling, Virginia, and Jupiter, Florida," and these facilities are capable of manufacturing active pharmaceutical ingredients and GMP-grade manufacturing to support biotech/pharma customers. My understanding is that Maravai is leasing these facilities, as opposed to owning them outright.

In the context of the Trump administration's tariffs on imported goods, the fact that all of Maravai's facilities are in the US and therefore tariff-free may attract customers who were formerly using overseas facilities.

In short then, it appears that Maravai's task in 2025 is to go out and attract more customers by offering more innovative and cost-effective solutions than its rivals.

On the earnings call, management notes that its acquisitions of Officinae Bio and Molecular Assemblies last year "bring us unique assets and capabilities to create a new ecosystem in the life science tool space," that its TriLink GMP products and services which utilise its Flanders 1 and 2 manufacturing facilities in San Diego "are primed for future growth in the genomic medicines market," and that its "Alphazyme enzyme business is adding new customers at an exciting rate."

Analysis - Am I Switching My "Sell" Call On Maravai Stock?

I think this is a tight call between "sell," and "hold." Personally, I think the companies face too many headwinds for a "Buy" call to be justified at this time.

Net cash position of just $22m, no more COVID vaccine-related revenues (despite the fact COVID vaccines will likely generate billions in 2025 - either vaccine makers are no longer using Clean Cap, or they have stockpiled sufficient quantities not to need to accumulate more), spare capacity at its manufacturing facilities that is a drain on financial resources, a depressed biotech market (particularly within mRNA and cell therapy, key clients for Maravai), meaning Maravai's customers have less and less money to spend, and a lack of tangible progress within the mRNA field of medicines. The company also has 570 staff, almost a third of whom hold advanced degrees, meaning they command high wages, and it competes against better-resourced and larger rivals.

These are tough challenges, and management admits it has been experiencing challenges in forecasting demand, and that it may need to implement further cost-saving initiatives in order to avoid running into financial difficulties.

Balanced against that are some positives, however. The company says it has "executed 11 new agreements in 2024, bringing our total to 43 license holders," and also estimates "clean cap market share at approximately 30% for clinical stage programs we track, and closer to 40% at the discovery stage, which should drive increased future participation in clinical programs."

My conclusion therefore is to award Maravai a "Hold" rating, upgrading my prior "Sell" call. In summary, it is clear that Maravai's business has suffered badly in recent years, as the business that made the company so initially successful barely exists anymore.

The Maravai LifeSciences Holdings, Inc. share price descent has been ugly, and I am not sure if we have hit the bottom yet. An exit to a rival via M&A is possible, but would any company spend $500m acquiring Maravai's business, which is now more about what may happen going forward than any intrinsic value in the business today?

With that said, I do believe that the biotech industry is currently in a trough that it can recover from, and a rising tide may lift all boats, including Maravai, as budgets increase and companies are prepared to take more budgetary risks.

I don't see that happening in 2025, but neither do I see this being the end of the road for Maravai. If I were a shareholder, I'd cross my fingers for better trading conditions, and keep holding stock.

data.title: Stock Picks From Seeking Alpha's March 2025 New Analysts
data.description:
Showcase Intro

In March, we welcomed 30 new analysts who published their first-ever article on Seeking Alpha. In this article, our editors highlight some of the best ideas from these new analysts and introduce them.

The first five introductions are spotlight features, including a longer excerpt from the analyst's article. The rest of the new analysts will be introduced after these with a more brief excerpt, and organized by rating from Strong Sell to Strong Buy. Each section includes details about the new analysts' interests and backgrounds, so you can get to know them a bit more.

With all the ideas and information shared in this article, I'd invite you to join the conversation and let others know what you think: are any of these picks worth following up on?

To our new analysts: welcome to the community! And please don't hesitate to share more in the comments to introduce yourself to our readers.

Top Stock Picks From Seeking Alpha's New Analysts

Julia Ostian | C3.ai Is Growing, But So Are Its Problems

Biography: “I’m passionate about finance and investing, focusing on business analysis, fundamental analysis, valuation, and long-term growth, especially in sectors like AI, fintech, finance and tech. I study finance and economy and have hands-on experience in equity research, financial modeling, and creating investment content. I actively analyze publicly traded companies with a focus on business models, earnings performance, and competitive positioning. I joined Seeking Alpha to contribute thoughtful, research-backed analysis that helps other investors make better decisions. My goal is to offer clear, unbiased insights into companies' strengths, risks, and valuation to help readers to build their unique opinion and investment strategy.”

Hold | “C3.ai (AI) is in a difficult position. On one hand, it has strong momentum from the growing AI market, solid partnerships with major enterprises, and enough cash to keep running for years without needing more funding. But at the same time, its revenue growth is only okay, not enough to justify high valuation multiple, profitability still feels too far away to even mention, and constant share dilution does not help to fall in love with this stock. The company trades at a premium valuation (P/S ~7.5x), yet it isn’t delivering the type of hyper-growth that justifies such a high multiple. Meanwhile, competition from Palantir, Microsoft, and Google threatens its long-term market positioning.

At this stage, C3.ai is not a buy for me, but it’s also not a sell. It remains a highly speculative AI stock with potential upside—but also significant downside risk if it fails to execute. Personally, I want to sit this one out. I will revisit my decision if macroeconomic conditions improve and if C3.ai’s financial performance shows real signs of acceleration. Until then, patience remains the best strategy for me.”

-

Cyn Research | Robinhood: Long-Term Potential, Short-Term Pain

Biography: “I'm a passionate investor who has been researching publicly traded companies for over 6 years. My primary focus is on identifying great businesses at reasonable prices and holding them for the long term. While I have a slight bias toward technology companies, I maintain a broad perspective, including opportunities in crypto. I take a global approach to investing, occasionally seeking value beyond the U.S. market.”

Hold | “Robinhood (HOOD) remains a fundamentally solid company with an impressive track record and outlook for growth and innovation. However, I believe a drop in revenue growth due to the aforementioned headwinds, such as decreasing interest rates and trading volumes, is currently not being priced in and could lead to significant multiple contraction. Therefore, I view the best course of action for early investors is to just hold.

There are also some aspects to consider in which I might be wrong that could not make the stock drop significantly at all. However, I believe that, with a beta of 3 and 2025 market volatility continuing, there should be an opportunity to buy Robinhood at a more favorable valuation and price in the short- to mid-term.”

-

Eliana Scialabba | Central Puerto: Energy In Evolution, Looking To The Future

Biography: “I am an individual investor with over five years of experience in personal investing, holding a PhD in Economics from UCEMA. I serve as Chief Economist at CEEAXXI and Director of Financial Development at Luxury Entertainment CrowdTech. My investment approach focuses on 'value' companies with solid long-term potential. I share my knowledge with the community by offering analysis to support individual investors.”

Buy | “Central Puerto S.A. (NYSE:CEPU) is an undisputed leader in the Argentine energy market. Additionally, the recent changes both at a macroeconomic level and also at a strategic level for the firm, in my opinion, position it as an actor of growing importance in the medium and long term, not only in the sector, but in the development of the entire transformation that Argentina seeks, and needs, to carry out.

Unlike most companies operating in the country, affected by the volatility inherent in the Argentine macroeconomy, CEPU's contracts with the government, denominated in dollars and long-term, are fundamental for the stability of the company's performance in the medium and long term.

Additionally, the good financial performance and low debt exposure allows CEPU to finance investments in industry and other sectors, as well as continue with its dividend payment policy, which, together with its solid fundamentals, I consider shows its growth potential.”

-

John Duncan | CF Industries: The Cash King Of The Fertilizer Industry

Biography: “John Duncan is an individual investor with over 25 years of experience, primarily focused on value investing using Phil Town’s Rule #1 methodology. While his primary focus is value investing, he occasionally places long-term bets on growth companies that he believes have developing competitive moats. To manage risk, John employs options strategies such as selling out-of-the-money puts on stocks he wants to own and using covered calls to reduce his cost basis in the stock. He analyzes opportunities across various industries, provided he can develop a strong understanding of the business.”

Buy | “CF Industries (CF), the world's largest nitrogen fertilizer producer, boasts an economic moat with low-cost operations and superior logistics, ensuring consistent shareholder value. The company's strategic focus on nitrogen-based products and disciplined capital reinvestment have led to substantial free cash flow and shareholder returns. Future growth is bolstered by ventures into blue and green ammonia, positioning CF ahead of regulatory trends and opening new market opportunities.

CF is a good investment because they lead the competition in most operational metrics, their long-standing low-cost moat is still intact, and they are trading as a large margin of safety to their cash flows and have a 10% yield on cash. CF also has a long track record of consistently returning capital to shareholders.”

-

Kirill Derevytskyy | Kyndryl's Transformation Is Real - And The Market Is Sleeping On It

Biography: “I am an aspiring equity research analyst with a passion for understanding businesses, uncovering mispriced opportunities, and generating alpha. My investing philosophy follows a growth at a reasonable price (GARP) approach, blending fundamental research with valuation discipline to find companies with strong competitive advantages, margin expansion potential, and long-term efficiency gains. Currently, I am studying Economics, Finance, and Data Science at Imperial College London, where I apply quantitative and qualitative frameworks to investment analysis. I have gained hands-on experience through equity research internships and portfolio management, refining my ability to analyze financial statements, model future earnings, and assess risk-reward profiles. Beyond academia, I co-founded Capital Compass, where I publish research on macro trends, valuation techniques, and investment strategies.”

Buy | “Kyndryl Holdings (NYSE: KD) is an underappreciated IT infrastructure and consulting services company that is going through a strategic transformation with massive margin expansion, free cash flow growth, and revaluation potentials. Starting from its IBM separation in 2021, Kyndryl has aggressively terminated low-margin contracts and rebranded as a digital integrator, benefiting from hybrid cloud, cybersecurity, AI, and IT consulting. However, despite significant improvements, the market continues to price KD as a “melting ice cube,” underestimating the potential for a revival and immense growth opportunities.

With the risk of a slowdown in the short run, already majorly reflected in a 15% price decline, I believe KD presents a unique asymmetric risk/reward investment with a cheap price for a company with rapid business momentum that dominates its industry.
